request.userInput:
I need help because several tasks are vague.

I have to "improve the simulator" for an AASMA prototype by next Wednesday. The teacher did not specify exactly what counts, but I probably need to clean the UI, add one experiment, and write notes about the result.

I also need to prepare for a programming languages oral discussion next Friday. I do not know what questions will be asked. I only have the lecture slides and two papers.

There is a small math exercise sheet due Monday. It says "quick review", but these usually take longer than expected.

I can work about 4 hours per day. I am bad at estimating vague tasks, so I want early discovery time and not everything pushed to the deadline day.

stopReason: Accepted by 5/5 agents with no critical critiques.

planning_window.reason: Covers the period until the final deadline for the programming languages oral discussion.

inferred_availability.assumption: Student maintains a consistent 4-hour daily work capacity as requested.

tasks.name: AASMA simulator improvement
raw_mentions: improve the simulator for an AASMA prototype
uncertainties: Length and depth of notes required

tasks.name: Programming languages oral discussion preparation
raw_mentions: prepare for a programming languages oral discussion
uncertainties: Relevance of provided papers to the discussion

tasks.name: Math exercise sheet
uncertainties: Actual time required versus the quick review label

assumptions: The student prefers to front-load work on vague tasks to reduce stress.
The student avoids late-night work to manage energy levels.
The student considers the math exercise sheet due on the upcoming Monday.

task_views.assessment: High risk due to vague requirements and a mid-week deadline. Requires immediate scoping to avoid last-minute panic.
concerns: Scope creep in UI cleaning
Uncertain experiment complexity
Risk of deadline collision with the math exercise sheet
recommendations: Dedicate the first 2 hours to defining the specific UI changes and experiment parameters
Complete the experiment by the end of the week to allow time for writing notes
Avoid leaving the documentation for the final day

task_views.assessment: Long-term preparation task. Needs consistent, low-intensity study sessions to avoid burnout before the final deadline.
concerns: Lack of specific question bank
Potential for over-studying irrelevant material
High cognitive load
recommendations: Review lecture slides first to identify core themes
Summarize each paper into key arguments
Simulate potential questions based on lecture themes

task_views.assessment: Urgent, short-term task. Must be prioritized to clear the schedule for more complex work.
concerns: Underestimation of difficulty
Potential to distract from the AASMA simulator improvement
recommendations: Complete this task as the first priority to ensure it is finished before the Monday deadline
Do not treat it as a quick review; allocate full focus time

agentViews.overall_comment: The schedule is tight. The math exercise sheet must be completed immediately to clear the path for the AASMA simulator improvement, which is the most critical deadline-sensitive task. The programming languages oral discussion preparation should be treated as a background task to be worked on in parallel once the math and simulator tasks are stabilized.

task_views.assessment: High-impact project component; requires clear definition to ensure the prototype meets academic standards for the AASMA course.
concerns: Vague requirements risk scope creep or insufficient depth.
The experiment component is likely the most time-consuming and critical for grading.
recommendations: Prioritize the experiment design early to ensure it is robust.
Limit UI cleaning to essential usability fixes to preserve time for core functionality.

task_views.assessment: High-stakes assessment; oral exams require deep conceptual understanding rather than rote memorization.
concerns: Lack of question structure makes preparation inefficient.
Risk of spending too much time on low-yield reading versus active recall.
recommendations: Focus on synthesizing key concepts from lecture slides.
Use the two papers to support arguments rather than memorizing them entirely.
Practice explaining concepts aloud to simulate the oral format.
suggested_subtasks: Review lecture slides for core themes

task_views.assessment: Low-weight formative assessment; important for keeping up with course pace but should not distract from high-impact project work.
concerns: Often underestimated; risk of taking longer than the 'quick review' label suggests.
recommendations: Set a strict time box to prevent this from encroaching on project time.
Identify difficult problems early and seek help if stuck.

agentViews.overall_comment: The current workload is manageable if the student prioritizes the AASMA simulator and the oral exam preparation. The math exercise sheet should be treated as a secondary priority to ensure the high-impact project and exam preparation receive sufficient, protected time.

task_views.assessment: The scope is currently ill-defined and likely underestimated. Splitting into distinct phases is necessary to avoid a bottleneck before the deadline.
concerns: UI cleaning can easily become a time sink if not time-boxed.
Experiment setup and data collection often take longer than expected due to debugging.
Writing notes is often neglected until the end, leading to poor quality.
recommendations: Time-box UI cleaning to 3 hours total.
Prioritize the experiment setup early to identify technical blockers.
Draft notes incrementally as the experiment progresses.
suggested_subtasks: Audit UI and list specific cleanup items

task_views.assessment: Preparation for an oral exam is high-cognitive-load work. Given the lack of specific questions, the focus should be on synthesizing core concepts from the provided materials.
concerns: Passive reading of slides and papers is inefficient for oral preparation.
Lack of practice questions makes it hard to gauge readiness.
recommendations: Create a list of potential questions based on key themes in the papers.
Practice explaining concepts aloud to simulate the oral format.
Focus on connecting the two papers to the lecture slides.
suggested_subtasks: Review lecture slides and summarize core themes
Read and annotate the two papers
Practice verbal explanations of key concepts

task_views.assessment: The 'quick review' label is a common trap. It is safer to treat this as a standard problem set to ensure the student does not fall behind.
concerns: Underestimating the time required for math problems is a common source of stress.
The student's low energy state may make complex problem-solving slower.
recommendations: Start the review immediately to identify difficult problems early.
Do not spend more than 20 minutes on a single problem before seeking help or moving on.
suggested_subtasks: Initial pass through all problems to identify difficulty

agentViews.overall_comment: The student's plan to work 4 hours a day is realistic. I have front-loaded the vague tasks to allow for discovery and adjustment. The total estimated workload is manageable within the provided timeframe, provided the student adheres to the time-boxing recommendations.

task_views.assessment: This task is high-risk due to vague requirements and potential for scope creep, which could lead to significant stress.
concerns: Vague scope increases the risk of overworking to compensate for uncertainty.
The deadline is mid-week, creating a potential bottleneck if not started early.
recommendations: Limit daily work to 2 hours to prevent burnout.
Prioritize defining the experiment scope immediately to reduce cognitive load.

task_views.assessment: Preparation for an oral exam is cognitively demanding and requires steady, low-intensity study rather than cramming.
concerns: Lack of question structure creates anxiety.
Reading academic papers is energy-intensive and should be done in short, focused sessions.
recommendations: Schedule study sessions in the morning when energy is highest.
Avoid late-night reading to protect sleep quality.

task_views.assessment: The 'quick review' label is often misleading; treating this as a standard exercise set is safer for planning.
concerns: Underestimating the time required could lead to weekend stress.
Cognitive fatigue from other tasks may impact math performance.
recommendations: Complete this task early in the week to clear it from your schedule.
Stop if you hit a wall and revisit it later to avoid frustration.

agentViews.overall_comment: The proposed workload is manageable within the 4-hour daily limit, provided the student avoids late-night sessions. Prioritizing the math exercise sheet early will reduce overall anxiety. I recommend keeping sessions short and focused to accommodate the student's low energy levels.

task_views.assessment: High risk due to undefined scope and lack of clear acceptance criteria for the UI and experiment components.
concerns: Scope creep in UI cleaning
Experiment complexity may exceed time budget
Lack of feedback loop with instructor before final submission
recommendations: Prioritize defining the experiment scope immediately
Allocate time for a 'discovery' phase to test the simulator before making changes
Set a hard stop for UI improvements to ensure the experiment and notes are completed

task_views.assessment: High risk due to the open-ended nature of oral exams and limited study materials.
concerns: Unknown question format
Potential for deep theoretical questions not covered in slides
Difficulty in synthesizing two papers without guidance
recommendations: Create a list of potential questions based on lecture slides
Summarize papers into key concepts rather than memorizing
Practice explaining concepts aloud to simulate the oral format
suggested_subtasks: Review lecture slides for key definitions
Summarize core arguments of the two papers

task_views.assessment: Moderate risk; the 'quick review' label is a common trap for underestimating effort.
concerns: Underestimation of complexity
Potential for blocking issues on specific problems
recommendations: Treat as a standard assignment rather than a quick review
Identify difficult problems early to allow for help-seeking

agentViews.overall_comment: The current plan is fragile because it relies on the student's ability to self-correct on vague tasks. I recommend front-loading the discovery phases for the AASMA simulator and the oral discussion preparation to mitigate the risk of late-stage surprises. The math exercise should be prioritized immediately to clear the schedule for the more complex tasks.

overall_strategy: Prioritize the math exercise sheet immediately to clear the schedule.
Front-load the AASMA simulator improvement to manage vague requirements before the mid-week deadline.
Distribute preparation for the programming languages oral discussion in short sessions to manage cognitive load.
Maintain a consistent 4-hour daily work capacity to avoid burnout.

compromises.resolution: Prioritized summarizing papers and practicing explanations over reading everything in detail.

known_weaknesses: The math exercise sheet might take longer than 4 hours if complex problems arise.
The oral exam preparation is based on limited materials and unknown question formats.
The simulator improvement relies on the student's ability to define scope effectively.

unresolved_critiques.issue: The simulator improvement lacks a feedback loop with the instructor.
unresolved_critiques.planner_response: The student is encouraged to reach out to the instructor early if the experiment results are unclear.

acknowledged_compromises: Prioritized summarizing papers and practicing explanations over reading everything in detail for the oral exam preparation.
Allocated specific time for discovery and scoping for the simulator to prevent scope creep.
critiques.overall_comment: The schedule is well-structured to meet all deadlines. The math exercise sheet is prioritized for completion by June 5th, well ahead of its June 8th deadline. The simulator improvement is front-loaded to conclude by June 8th, providing a buffer before its June 10th deadline. The oral discussion preparation is distributed effectively across the remaining days, ensuring consistent progress without late-night work.

critiques.issue: The AASMA simulator improvement task is front-loaded but lacks a buffer for the experiment phase, which is the highest-risk component for academic grading.
critiques.suggested_fix: If the experiment implementation on June 6th encounters technical blockers, prioritize resolving those over drafting notes on June 7th to ensure the core deliverable is functional.

acknowledged_compromises: Prioritizing the math exercise sheet early to clear the schedule for higher-impact tasks.
Using targeted summaries for the oral exam preparation to maximize efficiency given the limited preparation time.
critiques.overall_comment: The plan appropriately prioritizes high-impact work. The math exercise sheet is handled early to prevent it from encroaching on the AASMA simulator and oral exam preparation. The schedule is balanced, provided the student remains disciplined during the simulator experiment phase.

acknowledged_compromises: The plan successfully incorporates the recommended time-boxing for the AASMA simulator improvement to prevent scope creep.
The math exercise sheet is prioritized early to avoid the common trap of underestimating problem-solving time.
The oral discussion preparation is broken into manageable, incremental sessions that align with the student's energy levels.
critiques.overall_comment: The calendar is well-structured and realistic. The distribution of work across the available days respects the student's 4-hour daily capacity and avoids late-night work. The tasks have been appropriately broken down into actionable sub-blocks, which mitigates the risk of underestimation for the more vague project components.

acknowledged_compromises: The plan adheres to the 4-hour daily limit, which is appropriate for the student's low-energy state.
All work blocks are scheduled well before 22:00, protecting the student's sleep and recovery time.
The distribution of tasks avoids heavy cognitive stacking by alternating between project work and study preparation.
critiques.overall_comment: The proposed schedule is humane and sustainable. By keeping daily sessions to 4 hours or fewer and avoiding late-night work, the plan respects the student's current low-energy and stress levels. The front-loading of the math and simulator tasks is a sound strategy to reduce anxiety as deadlines approach.

critiques.issue: The plan lacks explicit contingency time for the AASMA simulator improvement, which is high-risk due to undefined scope.
critiques.suggested_fix: Insert a 2-hour buffer block on 2026-06-07 or 2026-06-08 to handle unexpected technical issues or scope adjustments before the final deadline.

acknowledged_compromises: The planner has successfully front-loaded discovery and scoping for the AASMA simulator improvement.
The planner has prioritized the math exercise sheet to clear the schedule early.
critiques.overall_comment: The plan is well-structured and addresses my previous concerns regarding discovery phases. However, it remains fragile because it assumes no technical blockers will occur during the simulator experiment. Adding a small buffer before the deadline would significantly increase the robustness of the schedule.

dimension_scores.rationale: The plan addresses all tasks and respects the 4-hour daily limit. However, the vague nature of the AASMA task means the exact scope of work (UI, experiment, notes) is not explicitly time-boxed granularly, leading to minor uncertainty.
All tasks are completed well ahead of their deadlines (Math by June 5th, AASMA by June 8th, Oral Prep ongoing until June 10th). This provides excellent safety margins.
The 4-hour daily limit is strictly adhered to. The workload is realistic, though the Risk Agent noted a lack of explicit buffer for the high-risk AASMA task.
High-impact tasks (AASMA simulator, Oral Discussion) receive significant time allocation after the low-stakes Math sheet is cleared early.
Excellent balance. Work is strictly limited to 4 hours daily and finishes early each day, respecting the request to avoid late-night work given low energy.

Good resilience due to front-loading the math task and starting the vague AASMA task early for discovery. However, the lack of explicit contingency time for the AASMA experiment (as noted by the Risk Agent) prevents a perfect score.

strengths: Excellent adherence to the 4-hour daily work limit and avoidance of late-night scheduling.
Effective prioritization of the Math exercise sheet, completing it two days before its deadline.
Good distribution of the cognitive load for the oral exam preparation across multiple days.
Successfully incorporated discovery/scoping time for the vague AASMA simulator improvement early in the schedule.
weaknesses: The schedule lacks an explicit contingency buffer for the AASMA simulator improvement, which has high inherent risk due to vague requirements.
The breakdown of the AASMA task into sub-blocks could be more granular regarding the experiment phase vs. documentation phase.
comparison_notes: The model successfully synthesized the specialist advice, particularly regarding front-loading the math task and managing the uncertainty of the AASMA project.
The resulting schedule is highly actionable and respects the student's stated energy constraints better than models that might push for higher daily hours.
evaluation.recommendation: The schedule is very strong, especially regarding wellbeing and deadline safety. For future runs, explicitly allocate a small buffer block (e.g., 2 hours) immediately following the implementation of high-risk components like the AASMA experiment to absorb inevitable technical delays.
